title: Pure Spin Photocurrent in Altermagnetic Photovoltaic Battery
authors: Qiang Li, Shibo Fang, Zongmeng Yang, Xingyue Yang, Jianhua Wang, Rui Peng, Lin Zhu, Shuhua Wang, Dexing Liu, Min Zhang, Dahua Ren, Mai Zhang, Han Zhang, Yee Sin Ang
abstract: Altermagnets, featuring momentum-dependent spin splitting without net magnetization, provide a promising platform for spintronic functionalities beyond conventional ferromagnets and antiferromagnets. Here, we propose an altermagnetic spin photovoltaic battery consisting of a nonmagnetic semiconducting layer sandwiched between two altermagnetic electrodes. Using first-principles quantum-transport simulations, we show that a V2Te2O/ZnSe/V2Te2O junction supports a pure spin photocurrent for opposite Néel vectors in the two altermagnetic electrodes, with spin-up and spin-down photocurrents equal in magnitude and opposite in sign. The effect persists under both linearly and circularly polarized light and remains tunable with photon energy and polarization angle. Our results establish a realistic route toward light-driven pure spin-current generation in altermagnetic junctions.
summary: This theoretical study proposes an altermagnetic photovoltaic battery to generate a pure spin photocurrent using light. By simulating a $	ext{V}_2	ext{Te}_2	ext{O}/	ext{ZnSe}/	ext{V}_2	ext{Te}_2	ext{O}$ junction, the authors show that an antiparallel alignment of the altermagnetic electrodes breaks inversion symmetry, enabling a pure spin current without a net charge current. This establishes a promising, light-driven route for pure spin-current generation in spintronic devices.
main_problem: The paper addresses the generation of a pure spin photocurrent using light in an altermagnetic photovoltaic battery, aiming for a light-driven pure spin-current generation mechanism.
main_result: The device successfully generates a pure spin photocurrent when the two altermagnetic electrodes are in an antiparallel (AP) Néel configuration, characterized by spin-up and spin-down photocurrents of equal magnitude but opposite sign.
method: The study employs first-principles quantum-transport simulations, combining Density-Functional Theory (DFT) with the Nonequilibrium Green's Function (DFT+NEGF) method.
model_or_system: The physical system is an altermagnetic spin photovoltaic battery structured as $	ext{V}_2	ext{Te}_2	ext{O}/	ext{ZnSe}/	ext{V}_2	ext{Te}_2	ext{O}$. Altermagnets are used as electrodes because they exhibit momentum-dependent spin splitting without net magnetization.
key_observables: Pure spin photocurrent ($	ext{I}_{\uparrow} = -	ext{I}_{\downarrow}$), photoresponse ($R$), and the dependence of the photocurrent on light polarization (linear vs. circular) and photon energy.
important_parameters: The effect is maximized in the AP state and is tunable by photon energy and polarization angle. The simulation uses a $	ext{V}_2	ext{Te}_2	ext{O}$ platform.
main_assumptions: The mechanism relies on the spin-selective photocurrent extraction imposed by the altermagnetic electrodes, and the central $	ext{ZnSe}$ region is assumed to be photoactive.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the device schematic, the operating mechanism for parallel (P) and antiparallel (AP) Néel vectors, and show the spin-resolved photocurrent response as a function of photon energy and light polarization.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the theoretical framework by detailing the physical system and the simulation methods (DFT+NEGF). It then presents the core results, contrasting the zero photocurrent in the P state with the finite pure spin photocurrent in the AP state, demonstrating tunability with light polarization.
why_interesting: While the primary focus is condensed matter spintronics, the use of quantum transport simulations (DFT+NEGF) to model light-matter interaction and spin currents is relevant to open quantum systems and quantum optics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of the altermagnetic spin pho- tovoltaic battery. (a) Device configuration, consisting of a nonmagnetic semiconducting layer sandwiched between two altermagnetic electrodes under illumination. (b) Operating mechanism for parallel (P) and antiparallel (AP) N´eel con- figurations of the electrodes. Red and blue arrows denote spin-up- and spin-down-polarized currents, respectively.

FIG. 2. Structural and electronic properties of monolayer V2Te2O. (a) Crystal structure of V2Te2O, with V, Te, and O atoms shown in gray, yellow, and red, respectively. (b) Real-space magnetic configuration of the altermagnetic state. (c) Fermi surface in the kx–ky plane, showing anisotropic spin splitting between spin-up (red) and spin-down (blue) channels. (d) Electronic band structure along high-symmetry paths, exhibiting momentum-dependent spin splitting char- acteristic of altermagnetism. The dashed line indicates the Fermi level.

FIG. 3. Electronic structure of the V2Te2O/ZnSe heterostruc- ture. (a) Atomic structure of the interface, with Zn, Se, V, Te, and O atoms shown in blue, yellow, gray, orange, and red, re- spectively. (b) Projected band structure, where red and blue denote contributions from V2Te2O and ZnSe, respectively. (c) Corresponding projected density of states (DOS). The dashed line indicates the Fermi level.

FIG. 4. Photoresponse of the altermagnetic spin photovoltaic device. (a) Schematic illustration of the device under illumi- nation, with a ZnSe semiconducting layer sandwiched between altermagnetic electrodes. (b,c) Spin-resolved photocurrent as a function of photon energy for parallel (P) and antiparallel (AP) N´eel configurations. (d) Polarization angular depen- dence of the photocurrent at E = 4 eV. Red and blue curves denote spin-up and spin-down photocurrents, respectively. θ represents the angle between the polarization direction and the vector e1.

Spintronics.note: The new paper is fundamentally about generating a pure spin photocurrent using light in a semiconductor junction, which directly falls under the scope of connecting quantum-optics concepts (light-driven) with spintronics (spin current generation in heterostructures).
